coredata.dc:title: Study of the effect of nitric acid on the structure and optical properties of alkali lignin-based carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Institution of Chemical Engineers (IChemE).
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Institution of Chemical Engineers (IChemE).
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Zhiqiang
Xia, Mingwei
Gao, Ying
Ma, Wanli
Chen, Yingquan
Wang, Xianhua
Chen, Hanping
Yang, Haiping
coredata.dc:description: Hydrothermal reactions can convert lignin into carbon dots, and the process often uses acids as additives, but the mechanism of action is not clear. In this study, lignin-based carbon dots were successfully prepared by HNO3-assisted one-pot hydrothermal method. The mechanism of the influence of the acidic environment on the structure and optical properties of lignin-based carbon dots was also investigated by changing the addition amount of HNO3. It was found that the particle size distribution of carbon dots collected was 1-5 nm, and they could emit bright blue fluorescence under violet light irradiation with the highest fluorescence quantum yield of 10.17%. HNO3 acts on the branched chains and ether bonds of alkali lignin, prompting the depolymerization of lignin and re-cross-linking and condensation to form lignin-based carbon dots. With the increase of HNO3 addition, the carbon core of lignin-based carbon dots gradually transformed from amorphous structure to complete graphene-like structure, and the emission wavelength of lignin-based carbon dots shifted from 517 nm to 499 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yield was increased from 2.61% to 10.17% by the effect of integrated N doping, which is of great significance for the analysis of the conformational relationship of lignin-based carbon dots, and for the guidance of the high-efficiency synthesis of lignin-based carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkali lignin
hydrothermal reaction
carbon dots
nitric acid
growth mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation, purification, and QY measurement of LCDs
Effect of nitric acid on morphology and structure
Effect of nitric acid on the chemical composition
Effect of nitric acid on optical properties of LCDs
Mechanisms of the effect of nitric acid on the formation of LCDs

And the technical support from the Analytical and Testing Center at Huazhong University of Science & Technology (http://atc.hust.edu.cn).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology and size distribution of LCDs prepared in different nitric acid addition.
Characterization of carbon skeleton structure and microcrystalline structure of LCDs prepared in different nitric acid addition.
XRD characterization of carbon skeleton structure and microcrystalline structure of AL and solid residues.
FT-IR spectra of AL and LCDs prepared in different nitric acid addition.
Characterization of the surface chemical composition of LCDs prepared in different nitric acid addition.
UV-vis spectra, PLE spectra, and photographs of aqueous solutions of the four LCDs under daylight and violet light.
Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectroscopy of four LCDs.
Time-resolved photoluminescence spectra of LCDs prepared in different nitric acid addition.
Effect of pH on the PL intensity and kinetic steady state test of LCDs.
Mechanism of the conformational relationship of LCDs by nitric acid.
Quantitative analysis results of XPS of LCDs prepared in different nitric acid addition.
Optimal excitation wavelengths, optimal emission wavelengths and QY of four LCDs.
Fluorescence lifetime decay curve fitting parameters for LCDs prepared in different nitric acid addition.

head.ce:title: Study of the effect of nitric acid on the structure and optical properties of alkali lignin-based carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, 1037 Luoyu Road, 430074, Wuhan, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aState Key Laboratory of Coal Combustion, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, 1037 Luoyu Road, 430074, Wuhan, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Energy Science and Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, Nanjing, 211816, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal reactions can convert lignin into carbon dots, and the process often uses acids as additives, but the mechanism of action is not clear. In this study, lignin-based carbon dots were successfully prepared by HNO3-assisted one-pot hydrothermal method. The mechanism of the influence of the acidic environment on the structure and optical properties of lignin-based carbon dots was also investigated by changing the addition amount of HNO3. It was found that the particle size distribution of carbon dots collected was 1-5 nm, and they could emit bright blue fluorescence under violet light irradiation with the highest fluorescence quantum yield of 10.17%. HNO3 acts on the branched chains and ether bonds of alkali lignin, prompting the depolymerization of lignin and re-cross-linking and condensation to form lignin-based carbon dots. With the increase of HNO3 addition, the carbon core of lignin-based carbon dots gradually transformed from amorphous structure to complete graphene-like structure, and the emission wavelength of lignin-based carbon dots shifted from 517 nm to 499 nm, and the fluorescence quantum yield was increased from 2.61% to 10.17% by the effect of integrated N doping, which is of great significance for the analysis of the conformational relationship of lignin-based carbon dots, and for the guidance of the high-efficiency synthesis of lignin-based carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are carbon nanomaterials with dimensions less than 10 nm in three dimensions (Sun et al., 2006). It has excellent optical properties, water solubility, biocompatibility, and environmental responsiveness, and has potential application prospects in the fields of fluorescence anti-counterfeiting, biological imaging, optoelectronic devices, and photocatalysis (Miao et al., 2020). CDs can be synthesized by both top-down and bottom-up methods (Hao et al., 2023). The top-down mainly includes the discharge method (Nevar et al., 2021), the laser ablation method (Kang et al., 2019), and the chemical oxidation method (Suryawanshi et al., 2014). These methods have harsh reaction conditions and are based on non-renewable carbon resources, so there is an urgent need to develop techniques for preparing CDs from renewable carbon sources using mild conditions (He et al., 2022). Bottom-up methods use organic matter or biomass as a carbon source to synthesize CDs through polymerization and carbonization steps, including pyrolysis, hydrothermal and microwave methods (Kang et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2019). The hydrothermal method has mild reaction conditions, convenient operation and simple process, which has advantages for the synthesis of LCDs. In the context of carbon peaking and carbon neutrality, researchers are paying more attention to the greenness and renewable nature of raw materials (Zhang et al., 2018).

Lignin is the second-richest organic substance with a natural aromatic ring structure, which serves as a potential precursor for carbon materials (Li et al., 2019; Si et al., 2018). Chen et al. synthesized LCDs by H2O2-assisted hydrothermal treatment of lignin and applied LCDs to bioimaging (Chen et al., 2016). Yang et al. prepared N, Mg co-doped LCDs by hydrothermal method using magnesium hydroxide and ethylenediamine as additives (Yang et al., 2021).Zhu et al. prepared LCDs with blue to yellow-green fluorescence expression using four organic acids as additives and applied them to anti-counterfeit printing (Zhu, L. et al., 2021). The preparation of LCDs via hydrothermal reaction has attracted more and more attention.

As the main additive, acid has an important influence on the formation of LCDs in the hydrothermal process. Ding et al. found that HNO3 acted as a cropping agent, nitroxide, and oxidizing agent to promote the generation of LCDs (Ding et al., 2018). Zhu et al. pointed out that acid additive plays a major role in nitrification and catalytic depolymerization during the formation of LCDs (Zhu, L.L. et al., 2021; Zhu, L.Y. et al., 2022). However, Lin et al. suggested that the acid mainly plays a triple role in clipping, doping, and surface activation during the formation of LCDs (Lin et al., 2023). Different kinds of acids also have diverse effects on the structure of LCDs. Liu et al. found that H3PO4 promotes the formation of phosphoric anhydride on the surface of LCDs and increase the charge density (Liu et al., 2021). Gu et al. pointed out that 3-aminophenylboronic acid introduces a specific defect structure for LCDs to have multiple fluorescence emission centers (Gu et al., 2022). Zhou et al. synthesized multicolor LCDs and summarized the effect of complex species of acids on the structure as introduction of functional groups, passivation indication, and control of particle size (Zhou et al., 2023). In addition, the amount of acid added has a great influence on the structure and optical properties of LCDs. Yang et al. observed that an appropriate amount of sulfuric acid would generate new surface states of the LCDs and improve the radiative compounding efficiency to increase the quantum yield (QY), but too much sulfuric acid would result in a sudden decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the LCDs (Yang et al., 2020). Pei observed that excessive acid addition affects the size of LCDs and leads to fluorescence bursts (Pei et al., 2021). However, most of the above studies only consider the doping/passivation effect of acid additives. Few studies have considered that it also affects the pH value of the solution environment, thereby affecting the structure and optical properties of LCDs. Therefore, there is an urgent need to further clarify the specific mechanism of action of pH on LCDs.

Here, structurally simple HNO3 was chosen as the additive and successfully prepared LCDs with high pH stability by one-pot hydrothermal method. The effect of a nitric acid addition on the structure and fluorescence performance of LCDs was explored in depth with varying acid added amounts. Furthermore, the growth process of LCDs in nitric acid and their conformational relationships resolved. It has important guiding significance for the efficient synthesis of LCDs and their structure and function regulation.

Alkaline lignin (AL, 96%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). HNO3 (67%, AR), H2SO4 (98.3 %, AR), and HCl (37%, AR) were purchased from Hushi Laboratory Equipment Co. (Shanghai, China). NaOH (96%, AR) and quinine sulfatewas (98.6%, AR) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Deionized water (DI) was used throughout this experiment. The diameter of the microporous filtration membrane was 0.22 um, prepared by Tianjin Jinteng Company. The retained molecular weight of the dialysis bag was 3000 Da, which was prepared by Xi'an UB Biotechnology Co. All chemicals were not subjected to any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation, purification, and QY measurement of LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: LCDs were synthesized using the HNO3-assisted one-pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, 1 g of alkali lignin was added to 50 ml of deionized water at room temperature with continuous stirring, until it was completely dissolved then several volumes of HNO3 were added dropwise. Once the solution was evenly dispersed, it was immediately transferred to a stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 180°C for 12 h.

After the reaction and cooled to room temperature, the mixture was vacuum filtered using a 0.22 um filter membrane, the solid residue and the filtrate containing the LCDs were collected separately. The solid residue was dried at 105°C and stored for further testing. Transfer the filtrate into a 3000 Da dialysis bag, and then move the dialysis bag into a beaker filled with 1L deionized water for dialysis purification. Change the water every 12 hours and continue dialysis for 3 days to remove small molecule fluorescent substances. Finally, the solution in the dialysis bag was collected and freeze-dried at around -60°C to collect the LCDs powder. Based on the ratio of the volume of HNO3 added to the volume of solvent (0:100, 0.8:100, 1.6:100, 2.4:100), we named the LCDs as LCDs-0, LCDs-0.8, LCDs-1.6, LCDs-2.4. Designate corresponding solid residues as S-0.8, S-1.6, S-2.4 (no residue collected at S-0). LCDs solutions were prepared by directly dispersing LCDs powders with different nitric acid additions in near-neutral deionized water (pH = 6.5∼7.2) for further testing.

The fluorescence QY of LCDs as the ratio of emitted photons to absorbed photons was determined by the relative method (Shen et al., 2015). Briefly, quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 as a fluorescence reference solution, and the reference fluorescence QY was 54%. To minimize the inner filter effect, the reference solution and LCDs solution were appropriately diluted and their absorbance was adjusted to ∼0.05. The PL emission spectrum was measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The calculation formula for the fluorescence QY of LCDs is as follows:
(1)
φ s
=
φ R
I s
I R
A R
A s
(
η s
η R
)
2

Where φ indicates the fluorescence QY. I indicate the integrated emission intensity, A indicates the optical density, and η indicates the refractive index (1.33 for aqueous solution). The subscript R indicates the reference sample of quinine sulfate, and the subscript S indicates the reference sample of LCDs samples.

Time-resolved photoluminescence spectra (TRPL) decay curves were fitted using the biexponential function R(t) and the average lifetime (τavg) was calculated according to the following equation.
(2)
R
( t )
=
K 0
+
A 1
e
(
− t /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
e
(
− t /
τ 2
)
(3)
τ
a v g
=
(
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
)
/
(
A 1
τ 1
+
A 1
τ 2
)
Where τ1, τ2 represent fluorescence decay lifetimes, τavg represents the average fluorescence decay lifetime, and A1, A2 represent the corresponding finger forward factors, respectively.

The detailed morphology was observed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA). The morphology of AL and solid residues was characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Gemini SEM 360, Zeiss, Germany). The graphitized structure was recorded by a Raman spectrometer with a 532 nm Nd: YAG laser source and a CCD detector (LabRAM HR800, Horiba JobinYvon, France). The microcrystalline structure of the solid residue was tested by X-ray diffraction (XRD, x'pert3 powder, PANalytical, Netherlands). Functional group information was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, and semi-quantitative tests were performed using potassium bromide pressed tablets with a 1:100 sample-to-potassium bromide mass ratio (FT-IR, Nicolet iS50R, Thermo Scientific, USA). Characterization of chemical composition information of LCDs surface layers by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS-ULTRA DLD-600W, Shimadzu-Kratos, Japan). The UV-vis absorption spectra of the LCDs were measured by a UV-vis spectrophotometer (UV-vis, Lambda 35, PerkinElmer, USA). Excitation spectra, emission spectra, time-resolved photoluminescence spectra, and fluorescence stability tests of LCDs were measured by a steady-state transient modular fluorescence spectrometer (QuantaMaster 8000, HORIBA, Canada).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nitric acid on morphology and structure
ce:section.ce:para: The appearance and morphology of LCDs in different nitric acid addition were characterized using HR-TEM with the result shown in Fig. 1 . As shown in Fig. 1a, the morphologies of LCDs are all in the form of uniformly dispersed quasi-spherical nanoparticles. Based on the results of 100 random sample measurements by Nano Measurer, the average particle size of LCDs increased from 2.4 nm (LCDs-0) to 3.16 nm (LCDs-0.8) and then decreased to 2.51 nm (LCDs-2.4) with increasing nitric acid addition. This pattern of particle size variation may be related to the N doping of nitric acid and the effect of its acidity, which will be discussed later. The particle size distribution also widened from 1-3 nm (LCDs-0) to 1-5 nm (LCDs-2.4). The HR-TEM images were further processed using Digital Micrographs. Graphite lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to graphite (100) plane, were observed in all four LCDs, suggesting the presence of highly crystalline graphite structures in the LCDs (Wang et al., 2021).

Respected to the morphology of solid residual, AL showed small irregularly shaped particles (Fig. S1), while solid residue (S-2.4) showed a lumpy structure with a larger size, which might be caused by the softening and aggregation of AL and its adhesion to each other during the hydrothermal process. In addition, very many micron-sized spherical pores were formed on the surface of S-2.4 after the reaction, and the reason for the generation may be related to the release of some soluble hydrocarbons and gases under the action of nitric acid.

The Raman spectrum results of LCDs is shown in Fig. 2 a, they are similar to those of biochar, and the D and G characteristic bands can be clearly observed. The G and D bands can be deconvolved into G, D, T, A, and D' bands. The G band is fitted as a Gaussian-Lorentz band, and the remaining four bands are fitted as Gaussian bands(Xia et al., 2021). The T-band at 1220 cm−1 is attributed to the presence of 7+ membered rings or heteroatoms on the basal plane; The D band located at ∼1350 cm−1 is attributed to the A1g breathing mode vibration of sp2 carbon activated by the presence of edges; The A band located at 1500 cm−1 is attributed to turbostratic graphene stacking caused by non-hexagonal rings; The G band located at ∼1590 cm−1 is associated with the E2g vibrational mode of sp2 carbon in the graphitic domain. The D′ band located at 1620 cm−1 is attributed to the turbostratic graphene stacking caused by other defects. Integration of the band (T+A)/(D+G) and D/G area was performed to explore the effect of the nitric acid on characterization, and the results are shown in Fig. 2b. I(T+A)/I(D+G) is the ratio of amorphous carbon skeleton to intact graphene skeleton; ID/IG is usually used to compare the graphene domain size of different biochars. The results showed that I(T+A)/I(D+G) decreased from 1.28 to 0.72 and ID/IG increased from 0.44 to 1.32 with the enhancement of an acidic environment. This suggests that the large-sized amorphous aromatic skeleton of LCDs gradually transforms into multiple small-sized graphene-like skeletons as the acidic environment increases. The promotion effect of a strongly acidic environment on the formation of the sp2 carbon skeleton of LCDs can be discussed from two aspects. First, increasingly harsh pH and high temperature and pressure reaction conditions will promote the removal of some aliphatic branches in LCDs and induce dehydration condensation rearrangement of aromatic structures (Ngu et al., 2016). Secondly, the oxidation and doping effects of HNO3 may introduce C=O and graphitic nitrogen structures into LCDs, which further increases the sp2 carbon ratio of LCDs (Zhu, L. et al., 2021).

The solid residue was characterized to explore more comprehensively the mechanism of the nitric acid addition on the formation of LCDs. According to the XRD spectra (Fig. 3 ), AL has a shoulder peak near 21.6°, which indicates that AL has an obvious amorphous structure, while the weak peak appearing at 34.6° may come from some inorganic residues of the AL purification process. With the enhancement of the acidic environment, the peak shape of the solid residue becomes sharp and gradually displaced to 25.2°, which indicates that the amorphous AL is transformed to the solid residue with partial graphite structure with the increase of the acidic environment (Zhang et al., 2019). This also laterally indicates the enhanced graphitization and crystallinity of alkali lignin hydrothermal products in the presence of acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nitric acid on the chemical composition
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 , the functional group information of ALs and LCDs was characterized using FT-IR. Detailed information on the absorbance of individual peaks for ALs, LCDs, and solid residues is summarized in Table S1. The peak at 3400 cm−1 and 1041 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration and deformation vibration of the alcohol hydroxyl or phenol hydroxyl group, and its intensity decreases significantly with the increase of nitric acid addition, which reflects a significant dehydration reaction of the LCDs. The peaks at 2880, and 2934 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of methylene, and methoxy (Wang, L. et al., 2019). The peaks weakened with increasing nitric acid addition until they disappeared (LCDs-2.4), suggesting that the strongly acidic environment may lead to C–H bond breaking and remodeling of the aliphatic groups in the side chain of the benzene ring. The peak at 1710 cm−1 is assigned to the non-conjugated C=O stretching vibration, and the increase in peak intensity correlates with the oxidation of HNO3. The peaks at 1605,1505,1460 cm−1 are aromatic ring skeleton vibrations of AL, and the peaks at 1505,1460 cm−1 disappear with increasing acidity. However, the intensity of the peak at 1605 cm−1 remains basically unchanged, suggesting that the carbon nucleus of the carbon quantum dot sp2 may be inherited from the native aromatic ring structure of AL. Located at 1367 cm−1 is C–N, indicating that element N was doped into the LCDs during the hydrothermal process. The peaks located at 1215cm−1 and 1134cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of C–O–C and C–O respectively. From AL to LCDs-2.4, the peak intensities of these are significantly weakened or even disappeared, suggesting that the breaking of ether bonds and depolymerization of AL may be the key steps in the formation of LCDs (Wang, R.B. et al., 2019). In addition, the number and intensity of peaks in the 1800-1100 cm−1 range decreased with increasing nitric acid addition, suggesting a shift of LCDs from isolated aromatics to larger conjugated regions (Gao et al., 2023).

According to the results of FTIR spectra of the solid residue (Fig. S2), the intensity of the C–O–C and C–O peaks decreased dramatically from S-0.8 to S-2.4. This further demonstrated the promotion of nitric acid in the AL depolymerization process. Notably, the solid residue undergoes severe dehydration, debranching, and defunctionalization reactions at lower nitric acid additions compared to LCDs. This suggests that solid residues are more sensitive to the effect of nitric acid during the hydrothermal conversion of alkali lignin.

Fig. 5 a shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of LCDs. The peaks at 168.5, 285.0, 400.1, and 532.2 eV belong to S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s respectively, the remaining weak peaks belong to trace residual inorganic element peaks within the AL. It is proved that LCDs are mainly composed of C, O, and small amounts of N and S. Deconvolution was performed to deeply explore the surface chemical composition of LCDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 5b-e. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the C1s spectrum of LCDs can be fitted into three peaks at 284.9±0.1, 286.3±0.1, and 288.9±0.1 eV, respectively corresponding to sp2 carbon (C–C/C=C), sp3 carbon (C–O/C–N) and C=O; the O 1s peak of LCDs were deconvolved to two peaks of 531.9±0.2 eV and 533.2±0.2 eV, representing C=O and C–O–C/C–OH; the N 1s peaks at 400.2±0.1 and 402.3±0.1 eV can be fitted to two peaks as amide nitrogen (N–H) and graphitic nitrogen (C–N) (Zhu, L.L. et al., 2022); the S 2p spectra of LCDs can be fitted as peaks at 164.4±0.1, 168.7±0.2, 169.5±0.2 and 170.2±0.2eV, which represent the thiophene structure (–C–S–) and –C–SOx (x = 2, 3, 4) units respectively.

The results of quantitative XPS analysis of LCDs prepared in different nitric acid additions are integrated in Table 1 . The results can be corroborated with the analysis of FT-IR. With the increase of nitric acid addition, the oxygen content of LCDs increased from 22.7% to 24.2%, and O/C increased from 0.3 to 0.33, which indicated that more oxygen-containing functional groups were formed in LCDs, which might be attributed to the oxidizing effect of HNO3 (Ding et al., 2018). In addition, it can be clearly observed that the sp2 carbon content increases from 40.6% (LCDs-0) to 49.8% (LCDs-2.4) with increasing nitric acid additions, which is in good agreement with the Raman results, suggesting the expansion of the conjugated region in the LCDs. The C-O-C/C-OH content decreased from 17.3% (LCDs-0) to 9.6% (LCDs-2.4), further demonstrating the promotion of lignin depolymerization by acidic enhancement. The increase in N content from 0.2% to 2.6% from LCDs-0 to LCDs-0.8 suggests that HNO3 addition can dope N into LCDs in the form of amine nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen. The decrease of N content in high nitric acid addition (LCDs-2.4) may be related to the heteroatom removal effect caused by the elevated degree of graphitization (Ma et al., 2019). The small amount of S in LCDs may be associated with residuals from the AL pretreatment process. Fig. S3 shows the high-resolution XPS maps of AL and solid residues, and the quantitative analysis results are summarized in Table S2. The addition of nitric acid increased the sp2 carbon ratio of the solid residues, and the O/C decreased sharply, suggesting that nitric acid promotes the cross-linking condensation of the solid residues and facilitates the removal of some unstable oxygen-containing functional groups. In addition, C=O is basically stable after the decrease, which is different from the change rule of LCDs, indicating that the oxidizing effect is not obvious to the solid residue. And the change rule of solid residue N is basically the same as that of LCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nitric acid on optical properties of LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6 , LCDs aqueous solutions are all light yellow liquids under sunlight. Under 365 nm violet light irradiation, the LCDs aqueous solution changes from light blue fluorescence to dazzling blue fluorescence with the increase of nitric acid addition. The UV-vis absorption spectra of LCDs show only a weak adsorption peak at ∼281 nm, which belongs to the π-π* transition of the sp2 carbon backbone within the carbon core. PL emission spectra of four LCDs aqueous solutions at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Figure S5. The emission peaks of all four LCDs were red-shifted to different degrees with increasing excitation wavelength. LCDs-1.6 was selected for representative analysis based on similar emission characteristics. When the excitation wavelength is increased from 280 nm to 500 nm, the corresponding emission peak of LCD-1.6 is red-shifted from 496 nm to 556 nm. The peak intensity increases and then decreases, reaching a maximum at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. This phenomenon can be explained by the excitation dependence of the emission wavelength and intensity of carbon-based fluorescent materials, which is mainly affected by functional groups, material particle size distribution, and emission state (Chen et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2015).

As shown in Fig. 8 , the bi-exponential model can fit the TRPL decay curves of LCDs well. Table 3 lists the specific fitting parameters for the TRPL decay curves of the LCDs, including the short-lived component τ1 associated with the carbon core eigenstates and the long-lived component τ2 associated with the surface defect states. The former corresponds to π-π* leaps in the sp2 conjugated domains within the carbon core of LCDs, and the latter corresponds to n-π* leaps associated with surface heteroatoms. The average fluorescence lifetime of LCDs was mainly affected by the τ1 component, and the percentage of τ1 tended to increase with the increase of nitric acid addition, from 80.32% (LCDs-0) to 84.21% (LCDs-2.4). The PL properties of LCDs have been reported to be influenced by a combination of carbon core eigenstates and surface defect states, which are generally dominated by the defect states. However, the fluorescence behavior of LCDs reported in this paper is dominated by the carbon core eigenstates and is continuously enhanced. This is of great significance for further refining the PL mechanism of LCDs. This phenomenon can be explained by the competitive relationship between nitric acid-promoted graphitization of LCDs and heteroatom doping. Under high nitric acid addition, the graphitization of LCDs is significantly increased and some unstable oxygen- and nitrogen-containing structures are removed, so that the carbon core eigenstates are absolutely dominant.

LCDs-2.4 was chosen as a representative to explore the pH dependence and photostability of LCDs. The pH of the LCDs solution was adjusted using HCl and NaOH, and the fluorescence spectra of LCDs at different pH values were recorded as in Fig. 9 a. The emission wavelengths of LCDs underwent a monotonic redshift with increasing pH, which might be related to the protonation and deprotonation of oxygen-containing functional groups in LCDs (Ding et al., 2018). The fluorescence spectra were further integrated at different pH to compare the relative fluorescence intensity of LCDs-2.4 at different pH. As shown in Fig. 9b, the fluorescence intensity of LCDs-2.4 increases and then decreases with increasing pH, and the intensity is maximum at pH 5.0, and the high fluorescence stability is retained in the pH range of 3-13. The sharp decrease in the fluorescence intensity of LCDs-2.4 at pH 1 may be related to the saturation protonation under strong acidic conditions (Su et al., 2019). Notably, the fluorescence intensity was retained by ∼90% even after 90 min of continuous excitation with a 75 W elliptic xenon lamp (Fig. 9c), suggesting that LCDs-2.4 have excellent photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of the effect of nitric acid on the formation of LCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above analysis, the influence of nitric acid on the formation mechanism was discussed and the structure-activity relationship of LCDs in Fig. 10 . First, HNO3 provides an acidic environment for the formation of LCDs, causing AL dehydration and debranching reactions. It also catalyzes the breaking of ether bonds, leading to the depolymerization of AL into oligomeric intermediates (Zhu, L. et al., 2022). These intermediates inherit the aromatic structure of the AL, which is re-crosslinked and condensed under hydrothermal action to form LCDs with amorphous carbon core structures. At this time, LCDs-0.8 has the largest amount of N element doping and exhibits the largest size. The increase of nitric acid addition enhances the acidity of the solution, the structure of LCDs is reconstructed, some unstable nitrogen-containing structures disappear, and the decrease of N doping causes the size of LCDs to decrease (Chen et al., 2023; Li et al., 2022). In addition, AL precipitates and aggregates to form insoluble substances in acidic environments. The insoluble matter is further cross-linked and carbonized under a hydrothermal environment to form a solid residue.

In the conformational relationship, the effect of acidic environment LCDs graphitization degree and doping structure is profound, which is the reason for the significant increase in the QY of LCDs. In the initial state, the particle size of LCDs is relatively large and does not form a complete sp2 carbon skeleton, the intrinsic state photoluminescence is limited, and the fluorescence performance is poor even with the introduction of N doping. As the acidic environment increases, LCDs further undergo strong debranching reactions and some hydroxyl groups are oxidized to carbonyl groups, causing an increase in O/C. The violent reaction further reconstructs the amorphous structure of LCDs, gradually increases the degree of graphitization, and enhances fluorescence. N-doping introduces structures such as pyridine, which changes the surface electronic configuration of LCDs, and the fluorescence characteristics are manifested as blue shifts in the excitation and emission wavelengths. In a more acidic environment, LCDs further condense and rearrange, reducing their size while obtaining a complete graphene-like carbon core. Its fluorescence is further blue-shifted based on the quantum confinement effect. In addition, the increase in the degree of graphitization will also eliminate some unstable doping structures, allowing LCDs to obtain more stable radiation recombination, and the fluorescence intensity will increase to the maximum (Liu et al., 2022). Also similar to LCDs, the degree of graphitization and doping structure of the solid residue changes similarly under harsher acidic environments.

Nitric acid has a significant impact on the formation, internal structure, and optical properties of LCDs. The LCDs were prepared by HNO3-assisted one-pot hydrothermal preparation, and the effect of an acidic environment on the conformational relationship of LCDs was investigated by adjusting the amount of HNO3 adding. In the formation stage of LCDs, nitric acid mainly acts as an acid to promote lignin depolymerization, exposing more reaction sites to enable lignin fragments to re-cross-link initially to form the carbon skeleton of LCDs. The doping, oxidizing, and graphitization-promoting effects of nitric acid increase the particle size of LCDs (LCDs-0.8). The continued addition of nitric acid increases the acidity of the solution, which in turn decreases the average particle size of LCDs (LCDs-1.6). At the same time, the presence of N in the LCDs changes, the unstable amine-nitrogen structure was weakened, while graphite N is retained in the LCDs and forms a more stable electron-donor structure, leading to a blue shift in the excitation-emission wavelength of the LCDs. When the nitric acid addition was further increased, the LCDs (LCDs-2.4) formed an intact graphene-like carbon core, which synergized with doping to increase the fluorescence QY by about 3.9 times. In addition, these LCDs can maintain the stability of fluorescence in the pH range of 3-13 and are resistant to photobleaching. This work provides insights into the formation and conformational relationships of LCDs under nitric acid addition and offers solutions for the conversion of biomass waste into high-value products with a low carbon footprint.

Zhiqiang Chen: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Mingwei Xia: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Ying Gao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Wanli Ma: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yingquan Chen: Conceptualization, Validation. Xianhua Wang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation. Hanping Chen: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision. Haiping Yang: Formal analysis, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There is no conflict to be declared.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their great appreciation for the financial support from the National Nature Science Foundation of China (52125601; 52306244). And the technical support from the Analytical and Testing Center at Huazhong University of Science & Technology (http://atc.hust.edu.cn).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ccst.2024.100218.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable synthesis of bright green fluorescent carbon quantum dots from lignin for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions
